Curriculum → Data storage and indexing → Relational data modeling and indexing

Lesson 1 of 5

Relational data modeling and indexing

Relational databases remain the default for strongly constrained data, multi-row invariants, and flexible querying with mature operational tooling.

25-35 min Data storage and indexing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Many interviews reward candidates who start with a relational model and only move away when scale or access patterns demand it.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Why relational databases remain powerful Use normalized schemas to avoid duplication at first2. Model for access patterns Add covering indexes for hot queries3. Operational trade-offs Use read replicas to offload analytics or heavy reads

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Many interviews reward candidates who start with a relational model and only move away when scale or access patterns demand it. 2. Why relational databases remain powerful Use normalized schemas to avoid duplication at first3. Model for access patterns Add covering indexes for hot queries4. Operational trade-offs Use read replicas to offload analytics or heavy reads
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Many interviews reward candidates who start with a relational model and only move away when scale or access patterns demand it.

What to say first

  • Start with entities, relationships, and invariants.
  • Index the hot read paths explicitly.
  • Discuss transaction boundaries and lock contention.
2

Study section

Why relational databases remain powerful

Schemas, constraints, joins, and transactional guarantees make relational systems excellent for products with strong correctness needs.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use normalized schemas to avoid duplication at first
  • Define primary keys, uniqueness, and foreign-key semantics clearly
  • Secondary indexes are often the difference between workable and unusable query latency
3

Study section

Model for access patterns

Schema quality is not just theoretical normalization; it is about making the common read and write paths efficient and obvious.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Add covering indexes for hot queries
  • Denormalize intentionally when repeated joins become too costly
  • Watch write amplification from too many indexes
4

Study section

Operational trade-offs

A strong relational design still needs connection pooling, query tuning, and a plan for read scaling and failover.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use read replicas to offload analytics or heavy reads
  • Keep transactions short to avoid lock contention
  • Explain when you would archive or partition old data

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Start with entities, relationships, and invariants.
  • Index the hot read paths explicitly.
  • Discuss transaction boundaries and lock contention.
  • Use denormalization only when it clearly helps.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Choosing NoSQL before the query patterns are known.
  • Adding too many indexes and slowing writes.
  • Letting analytics queries impact the transactional workload.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What data would you model relationally for an e-commerce checkout flow?
  • When is denormalization worth it?
  • How do indexes change write performance?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Replication and failover Data storage and indexingChoosing the right storage mix Data storage and indexing

Interactive topic lab

Interactive database decision lab

Use relational storage when correctness and query flexibility matter more than horizontal write scale on day one. The interviewer signal is not just “SQL”, but why SQL protects the risky part of the product.

Lead with invariants before schema details.Choose indexes from the hot query path, not from table structure alone.Move derived reads out of the transactional path before changing the source of truth.

How to explain it

High-signal talking points

  • Lead with invariants before schema details.
  • Choose indexes from the hot query path, not from table structure alone.
  • Move derived reads out of the transactional path before changing the source of truth.

Interactive diagram

Relational core with derived read models

Rendering diagram…

Keep correctness-sensitive writes in one primary system, then project read-heavy or analytical views outward.

Worked example

Inventory reservations and payment state need one authoritative write path

An e-commerce team expects short flash-sale spikes, payment retries, and strict “do not oversell” guarantees for the top 100 SKUs.

Recommended choice

Use PostgreSQL or MySQL as the primary store for orders, payment intents, and inventory reservations.

Why it fits

  • Transactions make it straightforward to reserve inventory and create an order atomically.
  • Unique keys and foreign keys stop duplicate checkout records from silently drifting apart.
  • Secondary indexes can keep merchant and customer support queries fast without introducing another source of truth.

If you chose differently

Starting with a document store simplifies flexible JSON payloads, but inventory correctness and reconciliation become much harder once retries and partial failures appear.

Interview-ready takeaway

Use the relational core for writes, then project read models into Redis or Elasticsearch if product queries grow beyond the transactional workload.

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Relational data modeling and indexing

Use the simulator to see when primary, replica, shard, or queue pressure should change your storage story. Apply it to relational data modeling and indexing so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Relational data modeling and indexing" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around why relational databases remain powerful and watch how it interacts with model for access patterns.Many interviews reward candidates who start with a relational model and only move away when scale or access patterns demand it.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • read p95
  • replica utilization
  • shard pressure
  • cache hit ratio

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

topology.flow Edit the topology and override script with quick inserts, inline diagnostics, and visible structure signals.

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 6: database · 21000 rps · 18 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Start with entities, relationships, and invariants.
  • Index the hot read paths explicitly.
  • Discuss transaction boundaries and lock contention.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Why relational databases remain powerful and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Start with entities, relationships, and invariants. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Model for access patterns.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Relational data modeling and indexing" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • What data would you model relationally for an e-commerce checkout flow?
  • Why relational databases remain powerful
  • Model for access patterns
  • Start with entities, relationships, and invariants.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
